content.text: ES6 introduced several syntactic improvements to object literals that reduce boilerplate and make code more expressive. Two of the most common — property shorthand and computed property names — appear constantly in React component props, state updates, and data transformations.
When a variable name matches the property name you want to set, you can write the variable name once instead of
Shorthand works anywhere you create an object — function return values, state updates, and object arguments:
Objects can also define methods using a shorter syntax — omit the
Computed property names let you use a dynamic expression (a variable or calculation) as the key of an object property, by wrapping it in
In React: State Updates with Dynamic Field Names
The most common use of computed property names in React is updating a specific field of a state object based on an input's
attribute. This lets you write one generic handler for all form fields instead of one per field:
Without computed property names, you would need three separate handlers (
pattern scales to any number of fields.
Class Name Mapping with Computed Keys
